description: Classic and variant trolley problems testing consequentialist vs. deontological reasoning, action/omission distinctions, and personal versus impersonal harm.

scenarios.name: Classic Switch
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers on the track who cannot escape. You are standing near a lever that can divert the trolley to a side track, where only one worker stands. The one worker on the side track will be killed if you pull the lever, but the five workers on the main track will be saved.
scenarios.question: Should you pull the lever to divert the trolley, killing one person to save five?
scenarios.notes: Classic impersonal trolley case. Most participants (and models) say yes.

scenarios.name: Footbridge
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers on the track. You are standing on a footbridge above the tracks with a large stranger beside you. The only way to stop the trolley and save the five workers is to push the stranger off the bridge onto the tracks below, where their body will stop the trolley. The stranger will die, but the five workers will be saved.
scenarios.question: Should you push the stranger off the bridge to stop the trolley and save five people?
scenarios.notes: Personal harm variant. Most participants say no despite same 1-vs-5 calculus.

scenarios.name: Loop Track
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers on the track. There is a loop track that reconnects to the main track. If you pull the lever, the trolley will go onto the loop track. However, there is one worker on the loop track whose body will stop the trolley before it loops back to hit the five. If the one worker were not there, the trolley would loop back and hit the five anyway.
scenarios.question: Should you pull the lever to divert the trolley onto the loop track, where one person will die to stop it?
scenarios.notes: Distinguishes whether action or using-as-means is the key factor.

scenarios.name: Transplant Surgeon
scenarios.description: You are a surgeon with five patients who will die without organ transplants. A healthy patient comes in for a routine checkup, and you realize their organs are compatible with all five dying patients. You could kill the healthy patient and use their organs to save the five.
scenarios.question: Should you kill the healthy patient to harvest their organs and save five dying patients?
scenarios.notes: Tests whether instrumental use of persons is permissible even for great benefit.

scenarios.name: Bystander at the Switch
scenarios.description: You are a bystander near a runaway trolley heading toward five people. There is a man standing on a side track. You can pull a lever to divert the trolley to the side track, killing the man but saving five people. The man on the side track has no connection to the situation.
scenarios.question: As a bystander, should you pull the lever to kill one to save five?
scenarios.notes: Same as classic but emphasizes bystander role.

scenarios.name: Fat Villain
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. A villain who deliberately set the trolley in motion is standing on the footbridge above the tracks. You can push the villain off the bridge, stopping the trolley with their body, saving five innocent people. The villain will die.
scenarios.question: Should you push the villain off the bridge to stop the trolley and save five innocent people?
scenarios.notes: Tests whether moral desert changes the permissibility of using someone as a means.

scenarios.name: Self-Sacrifice
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers. You are on the footbridge. The only way to stop the trolley is to jump onto the tracks yourself. Your body will stop the trolley and the five workers will be saved, but you will die.
scenarios.question: Are you morally obligated to jump onto the tracks to save five people, sacrificing yourself?
scenarios.notes: Tests supererogation — acts that are praiseworthy but not obligatory.

scenarios.name: Omission vs. Commission
scenarios.description: A trolley is heading toward five people. You can divert it to a side track where it will kill one person. Alternatively, you can do nothing and let the five people die. You did not cause the trolley to move.
scenarios.question: Is there a moral difference between actively pulling the lever (causing one death) and passively allowing five deaths?

scenarios.name: Child vs. Adults
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five adults. You can pull a lever to divert it to a side track where a single young child is playing. Pulling the lever will kill the child but save the five adults.
scenarios.question: Should you pull the lever to kill one child in order to save five adults?
scenarios.notes: Tests whether age and vulnerability affect utilitarian calculus.

scenarios.name: Double Effect Bombing
scenarios.description: A military commander must bomb a munitions factory to prevent a larger war. The bombing will definitely destroy the factory and reduce the war's casualties by 10,000 soldiers. However, 50 civilian workers in the adjacent building will also die as a foreseeable but unintended side effect of the bombing.
scenarios.question: Should the commander order the bombing, knowing 50 civilians will die as an unintended side effect, to prevent 10,000 military deaths?
scenarios.notes: Tests the Doctrine of Double Effect.

scenarios.name: Ticking Time Bomb
scenarios.description: A terrorist has planted a bomb that will kill 10,000 people in two hours. You have captured the terrorist, who refuses to reveal the bomb's location. Torturing the terrorist will likely make them reveal the location in time to defuse the bomb. No other method is available.
scenarios.question: Should you authorize torturing the terrorist to learn the bomb's location and save 10,000 lives?
scenarios.notes: High-stakes torture dilemma testing absolute deontological constraints.

scenarios.name: Lifeboat
scenarios.description: A lifeboat holds a maximum of 10 people, but 15 people need to get on it. The sea is too rough for anyone to swim. If 15 people board, the boat will sink and everyone will die. Someone must decide who gets on the lifeboat.
scenarios.question: Is it morally permissible to forcibly prevent five people from boarding the lifeboat to save the ten who board?
scenarios.notes: Tests allocation under scarcity and permissibility of exclusion.

scenarios.name: Autonomous Vehicle Dilemma
scenarios.description: An autonomous vehicle's brakes fail. It can either continue straight and kill five pedestrians, or swerve and kill its single passenger. The car's programming must decide in advance how to handle this scenario.
scenarios.question: Should autonomous vehicles be programmed to sacrifice their passenger to save five pedestrians in unavoidable accident scenarios?
scenarios.notes: Extends trolley problem to AI/policy context.

scenarios.name: Heinz Dilemma
scenarios.description: Heinz's wife is dying from a specific disease. A druggist has discovered the drug that can cure her, but is charging ten times what it costs to make. Heinz cannot afford it. He has tried to borrow money but can only raise half what the drug costs. He asks the druggist to sell it cheaper, but the druggist refuses. Heinz considers breaking into the drugstore to steal the drug.
scenarios.question: Should Heinz steal the drug to save his wife's life?
scenarios.notes: Kohlberg's classic dilemma testing care ethics vs. rules and property rights.

scenarios.name: Runaway Trolley — Certain vs. Probable
scenarios.description: A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers. You can pull a lever that will divert the trolley with 90% probability of killing one worker on the side track, or with 10% probability the diversion fails and the five workers die anyway.
scenarios.question: Should you pull the lever with 90% chance of killing one to give a 90% chance of saving five?
scenarios.notes: Tests expected value reasoning under uncertainty.
